Title: Turn a Feature Idea Into a Build-Ready Mobile App Spec
Teaser: Converts a one-paragraph idea into a tight functional spec a mobile team can estimate and build — screens, states, data, edge cases, and the platform behaviors people forget until the bug report lands.
PromptHint: Describe the feature or app idea and who it's for in [IDEA]. Add platform (iOS/Android/both), constraints, and what already exists in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a senior mobile product engineer who has shipped on both iOS and Android, and you write specs that survive contact with a real device — accounting for offline, permissions, and the back button before a tester ever finds them. The idea:
<idea>
[IDEA]
</idea>
Platform, existing app or greenfield, key constraints (timeline, team size, must-reuse components), and target users (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: list every distinct screen and the states each can be in (loading, empty, error, offline, partial, success), the data each screen reads and writes, and the platform-specific behaviors this feature touches — permissions, background, deep links, notifications, lifecycle.
Then write the spec. CONSTRAINTS: Specify each screen with its states, not just the happy path — empty, loading, error, and offline are first-class. Define the data model and what is fetched vs. cached vs. created locally. Name every permission the feature requests and the fallback if denied. Cover lifecycle and interruption: app backgrounded mid-flow, call comes in, low memory, process killed and relaunched, deep link into the middle of the flow. Note platform differences explicitly where iOS and Android diverge (navigation, system back, safe areas). Flag anything that needs a backend change or a third-party SDK as a dependency. Keep scope honest — mark anything that is a 'v2, not now.' If the feature handles health, financial, or location data, add one line that data handling and any user-facing claims should be reviewed by a qualified professional; this is not legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Summary' — what it does and the one user job it serves. (2) 'Screens & states' — table: screen, purpose, states, key actions. (3) 'Data model' — entities, fields, fetched/cached/local. (4) 'Permissions & fallbacks.' (5) 'Lifecycle & edge cases' — the interruptions handled. (6) 'Platform differences' — iOS vs Android notes. (7) 'Dependencies' — backend, SDKs, design assets. (8) 'Out of scope (v2).' (9) 'Open questions to resolve before build.'

Title: Map a Mobile UX Flow With Every State and Dead End Surfaced
Teaser: Takes a user goal and produces a step-by-step screen flow with branch points, error and empty states, and the friction moments where users actually drop — annotated so designers and engineers build the same thing.
PromptHint: Describe the user goal and the flow's start and finish in [GOAL]. Add the app context, known drop-off points, and constraints in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a mobile UX designer who has watched session recordings of real users rage-tap their way through broken flows, and you design for the tired thumb on a cracked screen with one bar of signal. The goal:
<goal>
[GOAL]
</goal>
App and audience, where the flow begins and what counts as done, known drop-off points or constraints (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: walk the user from intent to completion one tap at a time, mark every point where the flow can branch or fail, and find the two or three moments most likely to lose the user — friction, dead ends, or a screen that asks for too much too soon.
Then map the flow. CONSTRAINTS: Lay out the primary path as numbered steps, each with the screen, what the user sees, and the single action that advances them. At every step, note the alternate and failure branches — back, cancel, validation error, no network, permission denied. Mark empty and first-run states explicitly; a flow that only works with data is half a flow. Call out where you reduce friction (defer a sign-up, pre-fill a field, remember a choice) and where a confirmation or undo protects against a costly mistake. Identify the likely drop-off points and propose one concrete fix for each. Keep cognitive load low — one primary action per screen — and say where you violate that on purpose and why. Do not invent metrics; reason about behavior, not made-up percentages.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Flow overview' — start, finish, and the happy path in one line. (2) 'Step-by-step' — numbered: screen, what the user sees, primary action, branches (back/error/offline/empty). (3) 'Friction & drop-off map' — the risky moments and a fix for each. (4) 'First-run & empty states.' (5) 'Micro-decisions' — where you cut steps or added a safeguard, with the reason. (6) 'What to prototype first' — the riskiest part to validate.

Title: Write an App Store Listing That Earns the Install
Teaser: Produces a full, ASO-aware store listing — name, subtitle, keywords, description, and what-to-show — built around the keywords a buyer actually searches and the first three lines that decide the tap.
PromptHint: Describe the app, its main benefit, and who it's for in [APP]. Add target platform, top competitors, and the keywords you want to rank for in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an app marketer who lives in App Store Connect and Google Play Console and knows that most installs are won or lost in the title, the first two screenshots, and the line above the 'more' fold. The app:
<app>
[APP]
</app>
Platform (App Store / Google Play / both), main competitors, keywords you want to win, and tone (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: pin down the one benefit a buyer cares about most, the words they'd type to find it, and the objection that stops them from tapping install — then make the listing answer all three in the part that's visible before any tap.
Then write the listing. CONSTRAINTS: Front-load the value — the first 1-2 lines and the title/subtitle must convey the benefit and a primary keyword without keyword-stuffing. Respect the platform's mechanics: App Store has a separate hidden keyword field (use it for terms you don't repeat in copy) and Google Play indexes the description (weave keywords naturally, no spam). Write to a person, not an algorithm — benefits over features, concrete over vague, no hype words you can't back up. Avoid forbidden patterns: no fake urgency, no 'best app ever,' no claims about ranking or awards you don't have. Suggest the screenshot story — what each of the first three frames should show and its caption — since visuals out-convert text. Include a localization note: which strings matter most to translate first. If the app touches health, finance, or medical claims, keep copy factual and add a brief line that it is not professional advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Title & subtitle' — 2 options each, within character limits, with the keyword each carries. (2) 'Keyword field' (App Store) — comma-separated, no duplicates of title words. (3) 'Short/promo description.' (4) 'Full description' — scannable, benefit-led, keyword-aware, with a soft CTA. (5) 'Screenshot story' — first 3 frames: what to show + caption. (6) 'What's-new placeholder.' (7) 'Localization priority' — top strings to translate first. (8) 'Compliance check' — any claim to soften or remove.

Title: Build a 90-Day ASO Plan to Climb the Store Rankings
Teaser: Turns 'our app doesn't get found' into a prioritized App Store Optimization roadmap — keyword targets, metadata tests, conversion fixes, and review strategy — sequenced by effort versus impact.
PromptHint: Describe the app, its current store performance, and the goal in [APP]. Add platform, main competitors, and what you've already tried in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an ASO strategist who has moved apps from page-five obscurity to category-page visibility, and you treat the store like a search-and-conversion engine: rank for the right query, then win the tap. The app:
<app>
[APP]
</app>
Platform, current installs/ranking/conversion if known, competitors, and what's already been tried (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: separate the two jobs ASO must do — visibility (rank for queries with real volume and beatable competition) and conversion (turn impressions into installs) — and decide which is the bottleneck before prescribing work.
Then build the plan. CONSTRAINTS: Pick keyword targets by the trade-off of search volume vs. how hard they are to rank for vs. how relevant they are — chase winnable, relevant terms, not just high-volume ones. Treat metadata as testable: propose specific title/subtitle/description and creative experiments, one variable at a time, with a clear before/after to read. Address conversion separately — icon, first screenshots, ratings — since ranking without conversion wastes impressions. Build a review and ratings strategy that prompts happy users at the right moment and routes unhappy ones to support, without violating store policy (no incentivized or gated reviews). Sequence everything by effort vs. expected impact, and flag what needs a store-listing experiment tool vs. a code change. Be honest that ASO compounds slowly and rankings depend on factors outside metadata; do not promise specific rank gains.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Bottleneck verdict' — is the gap visibility or conversion, and why. (2) 'Keyword target list' — terms with relevance/volume/difficulty judgment and the role each plays. (3) 'Metadata experiments' — what to change, the hypothesis, how to read the result. (4) 'Conversion fixes' — creative and ratings work, prioritized. (5) 'Review strategy' — when/how to ask, within policy. (6) '90-day sequence' — phased by effort vs. impact. (7) 'What to measure' — the few signals that show it's working.

Title: Write Release Notes Users Actually Read
Teaser: Turns a messy changelog into crisp, on-brand 'What's New' copy for both stores — leading with what users gain, hiding the plumbing, and staying inside character limits and store rules.
PromptHint: Paste the raw changelog, commits, or list of changes in [CHANGES]. Add the app's voice, audience, and version number in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a product writer who has turned hundreds of git logs into release notes people stop and read, and you know the 'What's New' box is a tiny, recurring marketing channel most teams waste on 'bug fixes and improvements.' The raw changes:
<changes>
[CHANGES]
</changes>
App voice/tone, audience, version number, and whether this is a major or maintenance release (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: sort the changes into what a user will notice and care about vs. internal plumbing they won't, find the single most valuable thing in this release to lead with, and decide what deserves a line vs. what gets folded into a catch-all.
Then write the notes. CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the highest-value user-facing change in plain benefit language — what they can now do or what's no longer annoying. Group the rest by theme (new, improved, fixed) and keep each line short and concrete; never list a bug by its internal ticket. Match the app's voice — playful, plain, or professional — consistently. Stay within both stores' character limits and avoid anything stores reject (no pricing claims, no 'rate us 5 stars' begging, no competitor jabs). If a change requires user action (re-login, re-grant a permission, update a setting), say so clearly near the top. Provide a long version and a tight version, since the visible portion is short. Don't pad with 'we squashed pesky bugs' filler if nothing notable shipped — say what's true.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Headline change' — one line, benefit-first. (2) 'Full release notes' — themed, scannable, voice-matched. (3) 'Short version' — fits the first visible lines on both stores. (4) 'Action required' — anything the user must do, called out. (5) 'Internal-only changes excluded' — list what you deliberately left out and why. (6) 'Voice check' — one note on tone consistency.

Title: Craft Push & In-App Notification Copy That Doesn't Get Muted
Teaser: Designs the message set for a notification — push, in-app, and the fallback — with the trigger, timing, and personalization that earns a tap instead of a long-press-disable.
PromptHint: Describe the notification's job and what triggers it in [NOTIFICATION]. Add the app, audience, and any timing or frequency rules in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a lifecycle marketer who knows a single bad push can cost you notification permission forever, so you treat every send as a withdrawal from a finite trust account. The notification:
<notification>
[NOTIFICATION]
</notification>
App, audience, what triggers this message, timing/frequency rules, and personalization data available (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: decide whether this even deserves a push (interrupts the user) or belongs in-app or email instead, identify the trigger and the right moment to send, and name the one action you want the tap to lead to.
Then write the copy. CONSTRAINTS: Justify the channel first — a push must be timely, relevant, and worth an interruption; if it isn't, recommend in-app or email and say so. Write a tight title and body within push character limits, lead with the value or the personal hook, and make the implied action obvious. Personalize only where the data is reliable, and write a clean fallback for when it's missing (never 'Hi [FIRSTNAME]'). Set timing and frequency guardrails — quiet hours, a cap, and what to suppress if the user just opened the app. Provide a matching in-app version for users who have push disabled, plus the deep-link destination so the tap lands in the right place, not the home screen. Avoid dark patterns: no fake badges, no manufactured urgency, no guilt. If the message concerns health, money, or legal status, keep it factual and non-alarming, and note it is not professional advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Channel call' — push / in-app / other, with the reason. (2) 'Push copy' — 2-3 variants: title + body, within limits, each with its angle. (3) 'Personalization & fallback' — what's inserted and the safe default. (4) 'In-app variant' — for push-disabled users. (5) 'Trigger, timing, frequency' — when it fires and the guardrails. (6) 'Deep link' — where the tap should land. (7) 'A/B note' — the one variable worth testing first.

Title: Design a Permission & Notification Opt-In Sequence That Converts
Teaser: Plans when and how to ask for push, location, contacts, or tracking — with the pre-permission priming, the timing, and the recovery path — so you keep the user instead of burning the one prompt you get.
PromptHint: Name the permission(s) and why the app needs them in [PERMISSIONS]. Add the app, the moment in the flow you're considering, and your platform in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a mobile growth engineer who treats every system permission prompt as a one-shot, irreversible event — deny it once and you may never get a second clean ask — so you earn the yes before the OS dialog ever appears. The permission(s):
<permissions>
[PERMISSIONS]
</permissions>
App, the point in the user journey you're asking, platform (iOS/Android), and what breaks if denied (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: for each permission, find the exact moment the user is feeling the need it satisfies, decide whether to prime with a pre-permission screen first, and plan what the app does gracefully if the answer is no.
Then design the sequence. CONSTRAINTS: Ask in context, at the moment of value, not at launch — tie the request to something the user is actively trying to do. Use a pre-permission priming screen (your UI, which you can re-ask) before the system dialog (one shot) so a hesitant user declines your screen, not the OS prompt. Respect platform specifics: iOS App Tracking Transparency rules and one-time prompts, Android runtime permissions and rationale screens, provisional/quiet notification options where they exist. Write the priming copy to state the benefit to the user, not the business. Plan the denied path — the app must still work, and you need a non-nagging route to re-ask later (deep link to Settings, contextual re-prompt). Never trick or pre-check the user into a yes; no dark patterns. State the sequence as an ordered flow, not a single screen.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Ask order & timing' — which permission, at which moment, and why then. (2) 'Priming screens' — copy for each pre-permission screen: benefit, primary button, dismiss. (3) 'System prompt mechanics' — platform-specific notes (ATT, runtime, provisional). (4) 'Denied path' — how the app degrades gracefully and how to re-ask later without nagging. (5) 'Anti-patterns avoided' — what you deliberately did not do. (6) 'What to test' — the priming variant most worth experimenting on.

Title: Triage a Mobile Crash From the Stack Trace to a Fix Plan
Teaser: Reads a crash report or stack trace, reconstructs the likely cause, ranks suspects, and lays out the reproduction, fix, and guardrail — so an opaque Crashlytics spike becomes an action plan.
PromptHint: Paste the stack trace, crash log, or Crashlytics/Sentry summary in [CRASH]. Add platform, app/OS versions, and what the user was doing in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a mobile engineer who has chased thousands of production crashes across iOS and Android, and you read a stack trace like a paramedic reads vitals — fast, prioritized, and aimed at stabilizing before theorizing. The crash:
<crash>
[CRASH]
</crash>
Platform, app version, OS versions affected, device models, crash frequency, and what the user was doing when it crashed (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: read the top of the stack for the exception type and the last line in your own code, separate framework frames from app frames, and form two or three ranked hypotheses for the root cause before suggesting any fix.
Then triage. CONSTRAINTS: State the crash type in plain terms first — null/optional unwrap, out-of-bounds, main-thread block, OOM, threading/race, force-unwrap, deserialization, or a third-party SDK fault — and what that class of crash usually means. Identify the most likely line in the app's own code, not the framework frame where it surfaced. Rank suspects by probability and explain the reasoning for the top one. Lay out how to reproduce it, including the conditions (specific OS version, device, low memory, slow network, backgrounded) the report hints at, since an unreproducible crash can't be confirmed fixed. Propose the fix and, separately, a guardrail so this class of crash can't silently return (a nil check, a defensive default, a test, a feature flag). Note if the trace points to a third-party SDK and how to confirm that. Be explicit about what you can't tell from the trace alone and what data would resolve it. Don't guess a line number you can't see.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Crash in one line' — type and plain-English meaning. (2) 'Ranked suspects' — top causes with the reasoning for #1. (3) 'Likely culprit' — the app code frame to inspect. (4) 'Reproduction plan' — exact conditions to trigger it. (5) 'Fix' — the change to make. (6) 'Guardrail' — how to stop this class from recurring. (7) 'Unknowns' — what the trace can't tell you and the data you need.

Title: Run a Mobile Crash-Spike Triage After a Release
Teaser: Turns a post-release crash-rate spike into a calm decision: how bad is it, is it the new build, who's affected, and whether to hotfix, roll back, or hold — with the data to back each call.
PromptHint: Describe the spike — crash-free rate, when it started, and the release in [SPIKE]. Add top crashes, affected versions/devices, and your rollout state in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a mobile release manager handling a crash spike right after a rollout, and your job is to make a defensible call fast — stop the bleeding for users without overreacting to noise. The spike:
<spike>
[SPIKE]
</spike>
Top crash groups, affected app/OS versions and devices, rollout percentage and channel (staged/full), and crash-free user/session rates before and after (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: establish whether this is real and release-caused — compare crash-free rate against the prior baseline, check if the top crashes are new in this build, and scope who's actually hit (one OS version, one device class, or broad) before deciding anything.
Then triage the spike. CONSTRAINTS: Separate signal from noise first — a spike on a tiny sample, a single flaky device, or a pre-existing crash isn't the same as a regression in the new build. Quantify impact in user terms: crash-free user rate and how many real users are affected, not just raw event counts. Attribute it — is the top crash new to this version, and does it correlate with the rollout? Decide among the real options with criteria for each: halt the staged rollout, roll back, ship a hotfix, or hold and monitor — and say what threshold pushes you from one to the next. Plan the rollback or hotfix path including store review lag, since you can't ship instantly on either platform. Define what 'recovered' looks like so you know when it's over. Acknowledge what's uncertain from the data given; don't fabricate numbers.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Is it real?' — baseline vs. now, signal vs. noise verdict. (2) 'Blast radius' — who's affected (versions/devices) and how many users. (3) 'Attribution' — is it the new release, with the evidence. (4) 'Decision' — halt rollout / roll back / hotfix / hold, with the reason and the threshold that would change it. (5) 'Action plan' — exact next steps including store-review timing. (6) 'Recovery definition' — the metric and level that means resolved. (7) 'Open questions' — data needed to firm up the call.

Title: Design a Rigorous A/B Test for a Mobile Feature
Teaser: Turns 'let's test it' into a sound experiment — one hypothesis, a primary metric, the variant, the sample and duration logic, and the guardrails that stop you shipping a false win.
PromptHint: Describe the change you want to test and what you hope it improves in [TEST]. Add the app, the audience, current numbers if known, and any constraints in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an experimentation lead who has killed more 'obvious wins' than you've shipped, because most mobile A/B tests are underpowered, peeked at early, or measuring the wrong thing. The proposed test:
<test>
[TEST]
</test>
App, audience, baseline metric values if known, traffic volume, and constraints (release cadence, segments, tooling) (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: force the idea into a single falsifiable hypothesis, pick the one primary metric that decides the test, and reason about whether there's enough traffic to detect a meaningful effect before designing anything.
Then design the experiment. CONSTRAINTS: State one hypothesis and one primary success metric — resist a basket of metrics that lets you cherry-pick a winner. Name 2-3 guardrail metrics that must not get worse (crash rate, uninstall, latency, key downstream conversion) so a 'win' on the surface metric doesn't hide harm. Reason about sample size and duration qualitatively: bigger expected effect and more traffic mean a shorter test; a small effect on low traffic may be undetectable — say so honestly rather than promising significance. Insist on a fixed duration and no peeking-to-stop, covering at least a full weekly cycle to absorb day-of-week effects. Handle mobile-specific traps: version skew (users on old builds), staged rollout contaminating groups, randomization at the user (not session) level, and the lag before enough users update. Define the decision rule up front — what result ships, what kills it, what's inconclusive. Note that this is a design, and real significance depends on actual data; do not invent p-values or sample numbers.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Hypothesis' — one sentence, falsifiable. (2) 'Primary metric' — the single decider and how it's measured. (3) 'Guardrail metrics' — what must not regress. (4) 'Variants' — control vs. treatment, what differs. (5) 'Sizing & duration logic' — the qualitative reasoning and the minimum sensible run. (6) 'Mobile pitfalls handled' — version skew, randomization unit, rollout contamination. (7) 'Decision rule' — ship / kill / inconclusive, defined before launch. (8) 'Pre-launch checklist.'

Title: Pressure-Test a Mobile A/B Result Before You Trust It
Teaser: Acts as a skeptical reviewer on an experiment's results — checking power, peeking, novelty effects, and guardrails — and returns a clear verdict on whether the lift is real or a mirage.
PromptHint: Paste the experiment setup and results in [RESULTS]. Add the hypothesis, the metric, how long it ran, and the sample sizes in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a data-literate experimentation reviewer whose job is to stop the team from shipping a false positive, and you have seen 'statistically significant' lifts evaporate in production more than once. The results:
<results>
[RESULTS]
</results>
The original hypothesis, the primary metric, run duration, group sizes, and any segment or guardrail data (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: check the basics in order — was the test adequately powered, did it run long enough across full weekly cycles, was the primary metric chosen before the test, and could the result be a peeking or multiple-comparisons artifact — before judging the lift itself.
Then review. CONSTRAINTS: Question the magnitude and the confidence: is the effect size meaningful for the business, and is the result distinguishable from noise given the sample, or could it be chance? Probe for the classic ways mobile tests lie: peeking and stopping early when it looked good, testing many metrics and reporting the winner, a novelty or primacy effect that fades, version skew or contaminated groups, and randomization at the session instead of user level. Check that guardrail metrics didn't quietly regress — a conversion lift that raised crashes or uninstalls is not a win. Look for sample ratio mismatch (groups that should be 50/50 aren't) as a sign the experiment is broken. Distinguish 'real and shippable,' 'real but not worth it,' 'too noisy to call,' and 'invalid — rerun.' Be explicit about what you can't assess from the data provided. Do not manufacture statistics; reason about the design and the numbers given.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Verdict' — ship / don't ship / inconclusive / invalid, in one line. (2) 'Validity checks' — power, duration, pre-registered metric, randomization unit, sample ratio. (3) 'Could it be noise?' — the confidence/effect-size reasoning. (4) 'Hidden harm' — guardrail metrics and side effects. (5) 'Bias traps' — peeking, multiple comparisons, novelty effect — and whether each applies. (6) 'What's missing' — data needed for a firm call. (7) 'Recommendation' — ship, rerun cleanly, or drop, with the reason.

Title: Plan a Deep Link & Universal Link Strategy That Lands Users Right
Teaser: Designs how taps from email, ads, and shares open the app — universal/app links, deferred deep links for new installs, and the fallbacks — so a link lands on the right screen instead of the home screen or the wrong store.
PromptHint: Describe the entry points and where each link should land in [LINKS]. Add the platforms, your domain/app IDs setup, and known breakage in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a mobile platform engineer who has debugged why a marketing link dumped every user on the home screen instead of the promoted product, and you know deep linking is a chain where any weak link silently drops the user. The links to handle:
<links>
[LINKS]
</links>
Platforms (iOS/Android/web fallback), current setup (Universal Links / App Links / custom scheme, domain, app IDs), attribution/MMP in use, and what's breaking (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: trace the full journey for each entry point and each user state — app installed and logged in, installed and logged out, not installed at all — and find where the link currently breaks or loses its destination across that matrix.
Then design the strategy. CONSTRAINTS: Cover all three user states explicitly, because each routes differently: app present (open straight to the target screen), app absent (route to the store, then deferred-deep-link to the original target after install), and web fallback (a usable page when the app can't open). Specify the right mechanism per platform — Universal Links on iOS and App Links on Android over fragile custom schemes — and name the setup each requires (associated-domains / assetlinks.json, the apple-app-site-association file) without hand-waving it. Handle the post-install gap: a fresh install loses the original link unless a deferred-deep-link path carries the destination through. Plan link integrity: parameters survive, auth-gated targets prompt login then continue to the destination, and an unknown or stale link degrades to a sensible screen, not a crash or a dead end. Call out attribution if an MMP is involved so links don't break tracking. Note testing must happen on real devices for both fresh-install and installed states. Don't assume custom URL schemes are enough; explain why they fail.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Routing matrix' — entry point x user state (installed/logged-out/not-installed) and where it lands. (2) 'Mechanism per platform' — Universal/App Links vs. scheme, and the config each needs. (3) 'Deferred deep link plan' — how the destination survives a fresh install. (4) 'Fallbacks' — web page, store routing, unknown-link handling. (5) 'Auth & parameter integrity' — login continuation and param passing. (6) 'Attribution note' — keeping MMP/tracking intact. (7) 'Test plan' — real-device cases for each state. (8) 'Common failure points to verify.'
